title: GDPR Security of Processing — Encryption and Pseudonymisation Coverage
exclusions: Systems processing only anonymised or publicly available data confirmed by the data classification team; development environments using synthetic data with no connection to production data stores.
evidence: Encryption coverage attestation report listing all personal data stores with encryption algorithm, key length, KMS provider, last key rotation date, and any documented exceptions.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.32 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.32 (Security of processing) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.7: GDPR Security of Processing — Encryption and Pseudonymisation Coverage.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.7' running on index vulnerability and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Simulate an unencrypted data store containing personal data by logging a configuration event with encryption_status=disabled for a system tagged as PII-bearing; verify the search detects the gap and alerts the security team.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Log configuration events for all monitored systems with encryption_status=enabled and valid certificate references; verify no encryption gap alerts are generated.

dataSources: CIM Certificates data model, `index=vulnerability` (crypto-related findings), `index=network` (TLS metadata), database audit logs
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` dc(All_Traffic.dest_port) as ports, count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where All_Traffic.app IN ("http","ftp","telnet","smtp") NOT All_Traffic.app IN ("https","ftps","smtps","ssh")
  by All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.app
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| lookup gdpr_personal_data_systems.csv dest OUTPUT system_name, data_category, contains_pii
| where contains_pii="true"
| sort - count
| table dest, system_name, data_category, app, count
description: Article 32 requires controllers and processors to implement measures ensuring confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems — explicitly calling out pseudonymisation and encryption. This use case continuously monitors encryption-at-rest status for databases holding personal data, TLS enforcement on processing systems, and pseudonymisation coverage — providing the technical evidence that Art. 32 controls are operational, not just documented.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes network, vulnerability. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Create `gdpr_personal_data_systems.csv` listing all systems processing personal data (from Art. 30 register); (2) detect unencrypted protocols (HTTP, FTP, Telnet) to those systems; (3) monitor TLS certificate health for personal data endpoints; (4) track pseudonymisation implementation via application audit logs; (5) alert on any unencrypted connection to PII-bearing systems. Indexes required: `index=network`, `index=vulnerability`.
visualization: Table (PII systems with unencrypted connections), Pie chart (encrypted vs unencrypted traffic to PII systems), Bar chart (unencrypted protocols by system), Single value (% PII systems fully encrypted). Key fields: `app`, `count`, `data_category`, `dest`, `system_name`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Key rotation windows** — Scheduled cryptographic key rotations temporarily show gaps in encryption coverage as old keys are retired and new keys propagated; correlate with the key management system's rotation schedule.

2. **Development environment exceptions** — Non-production environments using self-signed certificates or reduced encryption for debugging may trigger coverage gaps; exclude environments tagged as non-production where no real personal data is processed.

3. **Legacy system migration projects** — Systems being migrated to encrypted storage may show partial coverage during the transition; verify against the approved migration timeline and risk acceptance.

4. **Pseudonymisation vs. encryption confusion** — Some systems use tokenisation or pseudonymisation as the Art.32 safeguard rather than encryption; the search may flag these as unencrypted when they are actually protected under an alternative technical measure.

5. **Internal transport encryption exclusions** — Data flowing within a single physically secured facility over a dedicated VLAN may be exempted from TLS requirements per the risk assessment; verify against the network security architecture documentation.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_personal_data_systems.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_personal_data_systems.csv')`.
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.32`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.32 (Security of processing) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.7: GDPR Security of Processing — Encryption and Pseudonymisation Coverage." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Create `gdpr_personal_data_systems.csv` listing all systems processing personal data (from Art. 30 register). The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 2:** detect unencrypted protocols (HTTP, FTP, Telnet) to those systems. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** monitor TLS certificate health for personal data endpoints. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** track pseudonymisation implementation via application audit logs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** alert on any unencrypted connection to PII-bearing systems. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Security of Processing — Encryption and Pseudonymisation Coverage," the aggregation measures control coverage — the output rows enumerate what IS covered, and an auditor's follow-on question ('what about the rest?') is answered by the gap between this result and the authoritative population.

This search uses `| tstats` against a CIM-accelerated data model rather than reading raw events. The performance delta is typically 10-50x over raw search, and for large indexes can be >100x. The acceleration summaries are maintained on a rolling window (default: 1 year, configurable in the data model editor) and are queried from indexer-local summary files rather than re-scanning raw events.

CIM acceleration dependency: this search FAILS SILENTLY if the underlying data model is not accelerated. It will return zero rows instead of raising an error, which is exactly the wrong failure mode for a compliance detection. Verify acceleration status before scheduling:

```spl
| rest /services/admin/summarization
| search title="<datamodel_name>"
| table title access_count summary.complete summary.earliest_time summary.latest_time
```

`summary.complete` should show 100 (or very close). If it's below 95, the acceleration is still catching up or has stalled — check the DMA audit log (`index=_audit action=dma_run`) for errors.

Aggregation choice: `dc()` is grouped by `All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.app`. This produces a summary row per distinct combination, which is typically what compliance reporting requires (one row per asset, one row per user, one row per rule). If downstream processing expects a flat event stream, avoid `tstats` — summary rows are not raw events and cannot be drilled into without re-running against the raw index.

When NOT to use tstats: if the field being filtered was added after the acceleration started, or is search-time extracted by an app the acceleration isn't aware of, the field will not exist in the summaries and the `where` clause will drop every row. Rebuild the acceleration after any field-extraction changes.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Network_Traffic` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Network_Traffic` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.7: GDPR Security of Processing — Encryption and Pseudonymisatio]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.7,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `app`, `count`, `data_category`, `dest`, `system_name`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table app, count, data_category, dest, system_name
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `gdpr_personal_data_systems.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_personal_data_systems.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.7: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`PII systems with unencrypted connections`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Pie chart (`encrypted vs unencrypted traffic to PII systems`):** Shows proportion across categorical dimension. Use sparingly — pie charts are poor at small-slice comparison; a bar chart is often clearer.

**Panel 3 — Distribution (`unencrypted protocols by system`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 4 — Single-value KPI (`% PII systems fully encrypted`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Zero results when acceleration is fresh:** The field you're filtering on was likely added after the acceleration started — rebuild the acceleration.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of security of processing — encryption and pseudonymisation coverage — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.
